title: Workload Management Pool Saturation (_internal)
spl:
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd (WorkloadManager OR "workload_pool")
| search ("saturated" OR "rejected" OR "queue" OR log_level=ERROR)
| stats count by host, pool_name, message
| sort -count
description: WLM pools prevent search starvation; saturation delays critical searches and alerts.
value: WLM pools prevent search starvation; saturation delays critical searches and alerts.
implementation: Define pool limits per SLA. Alert on rejections or sustained queue depth. Correlate with ad-hoc search storms.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
‚Ä¢ Install and configure the required add-on or app: Monitoring Console.
‚Ä¢ Ensure the following data sources are available: `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunkd` (WorkloadManager, `workload_pool`).
‚Ä¢ For app installation, inputs.conf, and Splunk directory layout, see the Implementation guide: docs/implementation-guide.md

Step 1 ‚Äî Configure data collection
Define pool limits per SLA. Alert on rejections or sustained queue depth. Correlate with ad-hoc search storms.

Step 2 ‚Äî Create the search and alert
Run the following SPL in Search (then save as report or alert; adjust time range and threshold as needed):

```spl
index=_internal sourcetype=splunkd (WorkloadManager OR "workload_pool")
| search ("saturated" OR "rejected" OR "queue" OR log_level=ERROR)
| stats count by host, pool_name, message
| sort -count
```

Understanding this SPL

**Workload Management Pool Saturation (_internal)** ‚Äî WLM pools prevent search starvation; saturation delays critical searches and alerts.

Documented **Data sources**: `index=_internal` `sourcetype=splunkd` (WorkloadManager, `workload_pool`). **App/TA** (typical add-on context): Monitoring Console. The SPL below should target the same indexes and sourcetypes you configured for that feed‚Äîrename `index=` / `sourcetype=` if your deployment differs.

The first pipeline stage scopes events using **index**: _internal; **sourcetype**: splunkd. That sourcetype matches what this use case lists under Data sources.

**Pipeline walkthrough**

‚Ä¢ Scopes the data: index=_internal, sourcetype=splunkd. Cross-check against **Data sources** above so indexes and sourcetypes match your ingestion.
‚Ä¢ Applies an explicit `search` filter to narrow the current result set.
‚Ä¢ `stats` rolls up events into metrics; results are split **by host, pool_name, message** so each row reflects one combination of those dimensions (useful for per-host, per-user, or per-entity comparisons for this use case).
‚Ä¢ Orders rows with `sort` ‚Äî combine with `head`/`tail` for top-N patterns.


Step 3 ‚Äî Validate
Confirm that events are present in the index and that the search returns expected results. Compare with known good/bad scenarios if applicable. Verify field extractions and index permissions.

Step 4 ‚Äî Operationalize
Add the search to a dashboard or set up alert actions (email, webhook, PagerDuty, etc.) as required. Document the use case in your runbook and assign an owner. Consider visualizations: Table (pools with rejections), Gauge (pool utilization %).
